quiz.title: Unit 6 Quiz 3, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Balancing Chemical Equations
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: Balancing Chemical Equations, Synthesis Reactions, Single Replacement, Double Replacement, Decomposition, Combustion, Law of Conservation of Mass

questions.question: When sodium reacts with chlorine, what is the correct balanced equation?
questions.explanation: Sodium reacts with chlorine in a 2:1 ratio to form sodium chloride. Sodium exists as Na atoms, not Na₂, and chlorine is diatomic (Cl₂). The balanced equation is $2\text{Na} + \text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{NaCl}$.

questions.question: Magnesium reacts with oxygen. The balanced equation is:
questions.explanation: Magnesium reacts with oxygen to form magnesium oxide. Oxygen is diatomic (O₂), and magnesium forms Mg²⁺ while oxygen forms O²⁻, giving a 1:1 ratio in the product. The balanced equation is $2\text{Mg} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{MgO}$.

questions.question: Calcium reacts with water. The balanced equation is:
questions.explanation: Calcium reacts with water to produce calcium hydroxide and hydrogen gas. The balanced equation is $\text{Ca} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Ca(OH)}_2 + \text{H}_2$.

questions.question: Aluminum reacts with oxygen. The balanced equation is:
questions.explanation: Aluminum reacts with oxygen to form aluminum oxide (Al₂O₃). Aluminum has a +3 charge and oxygen has a -2 charge, so the formula is Al₂O₃. The balanced equation is $4\text{Al} + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$.

questions.question: Iron(III) oxide reacts with carbon monoxide. The balanced equation is:
questions.explanation: This is the blast furnace reaction where iron(III) oxide is reduced to iron by carbon monoxide. The balanced equation is $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 3\text{CO} \rightarrow 2\text{Fe} + 3\text{CO}_2$.

questions.question: Sodium hydroxide reacts with hydrochloric acid. The balanced equation is:
questions.explanation: This is an acid-base neutralization reaction. Sodium hydroxide and hydrochloric acid react in a 1:1 ratio to produce sodium chloride and water: $\text{NaOH} + \text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{NaCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$.

questions.question: Zinc reacts with sulfur. The balanced equation is:
questions.explanation: Zinc reacts with sulfur to form zinc sulfide. Both zinc and sulfur exist as solid elements, and they combine in a 1:1 ratio because Zn²⁺ and S²⁻ balance charges. The balanced equation is $\text{Zn} + \text{S} \rightarrow \text{ZnS}$.

questions.question: Copper reacts with silver nitrate. The balanced equation is:
questions.explanation: Copper displaces silver from silver nitrate in a single replacement reaction. Copper forms Cu²⁺, so it needs two nitrate ions. The balanced equation is $\text{Cu} + 2\text{AgNO}_3 \rightarrow \text{Cu(NO}_3)_2 + 2\text{Ag}$.

questions.question: Barium chloride reacts with sodium sulfate. The balanced equation is:
questions.explanation: This is a double replacement reaction forming barium sulfate (precipitate) and sodium chloride. The balanced equation is $\text{BaCl}_2 + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{BaSO}_4 + 2\text{NaCl}$.

questions.question: Potassium iodide reacts with lead(II) nitrate. The balanced equation is:
questions.explanation: This double replacement reaction produces lead(II) iodide (yellow precipitate) and potassium nitrate. Lead(II) requires two iodide ions, so two KI are needed. The balanced equation is $2\text{KI} + \text{Pb(NO}_3)_2 \rightarrow \text{PbI}_2 + 2\text{KNO}_3$.

questions.question: Aluminum reacts with hydrochloric acid. The balanced equation is:
questions.explanation: Aluminum reacts with hydrochloric acid to produce aluminum chloride and hydrogen gas. Aluminum forms Al³⁺, so it needs three chloride ions. To balance, two Al atoms produce 6 HCl needed, yielding 3 H₂ molecules: $2\text{Al} + 6\text{HCl} \rightarrow 2\text{AlCl}_3 + 3\text{H}_2$.

questions.question: Sodium reacts with water. The balanced equation is:
questions.explanation: Sodium reacts vigorously with water to produce sodium hydroxide and hydrogen gas. The balanced equation is $2\text{Na} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{NaOH} + \text{H}_2$.

questions.question: Calcium carbonate decomposes upon heating. The balanced equation is:
questions.explanation: Calcium carbonate decomposes when heated to form calcium oxide (quicklime) and carbon dioxide. The balanced equation is $\text{CaCO}_3 \rightarrow \text{CaO} + \text{CO}_2$.

questions.question: Iron reacts with copper(II) sulfate. The balanced equation is:
questions.explanation: Iron displaces copper from copper(II) sulfate in a single replacement reaction. Iron forms Fe²⁺, so it replaces Cu²⁺ in a 1:1 ratio: $\text{Fe} + \text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{FeSO}_4 + \text{Cu}$.

questions.question: Lead(II) nitrate decomposes upon heating. The balanced equation is:
questions.explanation: Lead(II) nitrate decomposes upon heating to form lead(II) oxide, nitrogen dioxide, and oxygen. The balanced equation is $2\text{Pb(NO}_3)_2 \rightarrow 2\text{PbO} + 4\text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2$.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered balancing chemical equations! ⚖️🧪
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review equations with polyatomic ions and combustion reactions.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on balancing atoms one element at a time.
scoreMessages.0: Please review the Law of Conservation of Mass and practice balancing simple equations first.
